Perform technical recalibration on alternating nights (Monday, Wednesday, Friday, Sunday) at 06:00 after naturalistic sleep conclusion, treating electrode hygiene as a non-negotiable safety and data-quality checkpoint that participants accept as part of the residential protocol.

Deploy a hybrid model in which participants with stable skin conductance and low electrode-drift variance receive weekly recalibration, while those showing signal instability move to three-night frequency, personalizing the schedule to individual physiological response rather than applying a uniform rule.
levers.review: The core tension is that perfect signal fidelity collides with naturalistic sleep behavior — clinical EEG protocols do not tolerate drift, but frequent handling disrupts the domestic feel that suppresses behavioral modification. None of the options directly measure whether recalibration interruptions themselves trigger compensatory parasomnias on post-recalibration nights, a confound that could bias within-person phenotyping.

levers.name: Technician Real-Time Annotation Authority and Escalation Protocol
levers.lever_classification: execution — who decides in real time whether to escalate to full PSG and on what criteria
levers.consequences: Empowering the night technician to flag candidate events and recommend escalation to full PSG in real time improves event capture sensitivity and allows clinical-grade documentation of recurrent episodes within the same night. However, over-flagging drains the escalation budget, increases participant sleep disruption, and creates annotation bottlenecks; under-flagging misses rare events and undermines the entire event-yield model for Aim 2. Technician fatigue and training consistency also directly affect false-positive rates.
options: Establish a two-tier threshold: technician flags all candidate events (movement anomalies, EEG arousals, vocalization) in the morning review meeting with the PI, who then decides escalation based on contextual severity, timing, and prior participant history — technician is an observational scout, not a diagnostic gatekeeper.

Embed a co-resident clinical sleep specialist (1 FTE, $$ additional cost) on a rotating weekly schedule to co-review technician flags in real time during the night and make escalation calls collaboratively, reducing single-observer bias and improving diagnostic accuracy but introducing coordination overhead.

Implement a semi-automated preliminary triage algorithm trained on the first 10 participants' dual-scored data, which assigns a probabilistic event likelihood score to technician-flagged candidates overnight; technician escalates only candidates above a calibrated threshold, creating a reproducible and objective escalation rule.
levers.review: The underlying tension is that the technician is both a safety monitor and a data-collection gatekeeper, yet their training, fatigue state, and consistency are never formally measured or reported in the success criteria. None of the options mandate inter-technician reliability audits or explicit performance metrics for false-positive and false-negative rates, so escalation behavior can drift undetected across the 3-year period.

levers.name: Matched Control Group Recruitment and Naturalistic Behavior Expectation
levers.lever_classification: methodology — whether and how extensively to recruit healthy comparison participants
levers.consequences: Including a parallel control group (matched for age, sex, medication, comorbidity) on identical residential monitoring enables rigorous benchmarking of the event-triage tool and separates true parasomnia signatures from normal nocturnal movement and arousal noise. However, controls require comparable recruitment effort, per-night compensation, and annotation labor (€80/night × ~30 control participants × ~5 nights average = ~€12K in direct costs plus staff time), and their stays may be unproductive because they are less likely to produce detectable candidate events, wasting resources.
options: Recruit and admit controls in a rolling fashion matched to the case population as the study progresses; aim for a 1:1 case-to-control ratio (approximately 50–70 cases + 50–70 controls over 3 years), accepting the cost as essential to Aim 3 tool validation and reporting control-group false-positive baselines explicitly.

Reduce controls to a smaller reference cohort (10–15 individuals) admitted once during year two, monitored for extended 7–10 day periods to yield a rich but limited comparison dataset; use their baseline patterns as a static anchor rather than scaling controls proportionally with cases.

Partner with an established sleep center or consortium sleep lab network to source baseline control data from their existing normative databases or volunteer control studies, reducing the residential burden but risking temporal and population mismatch with the Bonn case cohort and potential loss of site-specific environmental context.
levers.review: The core tension is that rigorous tool validation demands matched controls, but the residential model's economics and recruitment capacity may not support a true 1:1 ratio without budget reallocation or compromise. None of the options explicitly addresses the risk that controls, lacking parasomnia burden, may show lower engagement or compliance during stay, biasing their nocturnal behavior away from a true 'normal' baseline.

levers.name: Participant Extended-Stay Eligibility and Event-Capture Certainty Tolerance
levers.lever_classification: execution — how long to allow stays and under what criteria to extend beyond the initial window
levers.consequences: Extending stays up to 10 weeks total (2 additional weeks beyond the baseline 2–8 week window) for participants with zero captured events improves event-yield statistics and allows a second chance at observing rare episodes. However, extension creates participant fatigue, disrupts their home life, increases facility occupancy unpredictability (complicating recruitment scheduling), and can stretch staffing and annotation resources. The protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions is a backstop but does not prevent resource-bleeding before the ceiling is triggered.
options: Cap initial stays at 4 weeks for all participants and offer extension only if zero events are captured and the participant explicitly consents; extension admits are prioritized for scheduling flexibility, scheduled outside high-recruitment windows, and monitored for fatigue-related behavioral changes that might artificially increase false-positive sensor triggers.

Implement a pre-stay event-probability stratification based on participant self-reported episode frequency and prior polysomnography findings; only participants in the lowest event-probability quartile are offered extension, while high-frequency participants are monitored for a standard 3-week window to avoid over-stay of known high-yielders.

Accept a 4-week hard ceiling for all participants regardless of event capture; view unproductive stays as a methodological cost of studying rare phenomena, and instead increase recruitment target to 80–100 NREM parasomnia participants over 3 years to compensate for event-yield variance, accepting higher total participant throughput as the trade-off.
levers.review: The core tension is between statistical power (more events, longer observation) and resource efficiency (bounded costs, staff sustainability). The protocol's 20% unproductive-admission ceiling is a governance tripwire, but none of the options establish a clear stopping rule or real-time adjustment mechanism to detect when the ceiling is approaching, risking reactive crisis rather than proactive rebalancing.

levers.name: Inter-Rater Reliability Scoring Model and Annotation Workload Distribution
levers.lever_classification: methodology — how many raters must independently score each event and how to handle disagreement
levers.consequences: Dual-rater independent scoring of all captured events against AASM criteria is the gold standard for event adjudication and supports Aim 3 tool benchmarking. However, scoring is labor-intensive and expensive (approximately €50–80K over 3 years in independent scorer fees and coordination), and prolonged inter-rater disagreement on borderline events can delay publication and create ambiguity in phenotyping. Efficiency pressures may incentivize expedited single-rater decisions for high-confidence events, risking systematic bias.
options: Establish a tiered scoring workflow: all events receive dual-independent scoring; if agreement exceeds 85% (simple concordance), the event is adjudicated; if agreement is 60–85%, a third referee (the PI or senior co-investigator) breaks the tie; below 60%, the event is flagged as ambiguous and documented as such in the dataset, allowing secondary analyses to include or exclude borderline cases.

Contract a professional independent polysomnography scoring service (e.g., a centralized sleep lab or consortium) to perform one leg of dual scoring, standardizing rater training and reducing annotation bottlenecks, with the Bonn team performing the second round of scoring in-house; the external scorer introduces cost but offloads variance and permits parallel workflow.

Implement a single-rater model with structured consensus meetings: the primary team rater scores all events in real time; a second rater audits a random 20% sample and attends weekly consensus rounds where discrepancies are discussed and a single agreed score is assigned; inter-rater agreement is monitored on the audited sample and reported, reducing total scoring hours while maintaining quality checks.
levers.review: The core tension is that dual-scoring is scientifically rigorous but expensive and slow, while single-rater plus auditing is faster and cheaper but risks undetected systematic bias from the primary rater's interpretation of borderline cases. None of the options explicitly measure or report rater drift over time — a single rater's criteria may shift from month 3 to month 30, introducing hidden heterogeneity in the scoring stream.

levers.name: Facility Expansion Trigger Logic and Renovation Investment Sequencing
levers.lever_classification: operations — when and how to commit to expanding from 8 to 12 suites
levers.consequences: The plan defers expansion from 8 to 12 suites pending proof of data quality, false-alarm rates, staffing sustainability, and annotation throughput by month 18–24. Expansion requires an additional €150–200K renovation and equipment, with a 2–3 month lead time. Deferring expansion preserves cash flow and reduces risk of over-capacity if recruitment or event yield underperforms, but committing too late to expansion misses the window for year-three recruitment ramp-up, potentially shortfalling the 50–70 participant target.
options: Establish an explicit expansion decision point at month 20: if enrollment reaches ≥40 NREM participants and event-capture rate exceeds 65% by that date, approve renovation; if not, maintain 8-suite operations and extend the recruitment window into year four, trading facility scale for extended recruitment.

Begin renovation design and contractor engagement in year one (month 6–9) so that expansion can commence construction in month 18–20 without timeline risk; front-load soft costs (architecture, permitting) while deferring long-lead equipment orders until the month-18 go/no-go decision, splitting the decision into design readiness and activation readiness.

Negotiate with the property landlord a pre-signed lease option for 4 additional suites (suites 9–12) with renovation costs and timeline locked in year-one contract, conditional on activation by month 24; this secures the expansion pathway without cash commitment and allows the final activation decision to be made at month 18 with certainty of cost and timeline.
levers.review: The core tension is that expansion decisions are based on recruitment and event-yield thresholds, but these thresholds are predictive estimates that may not reflect data quality or staffing strain — a facility could hit enrollment targets while suffering from escalating annotation backlogs or false-alarm rates. None of the options establish leading indicators (staff overtime hours, annotation turnaround time, equipment downtime) that could trigger a de-facto expansion pause even if enrollment targets are nominally met.

levers.name: Bed Partner Co-Interview Depth and Collateral History Weighting in Eligibility
levers.lever_classification: governance — whether collateral history from bed partners is a mandatory or optional part of recruitment adjudication
levers.consequences: Recruiting and interviewing the bed partner (or household member) at pre-admission provides rich collateral history of episode frequency, morphology, and triggers, improving diagnostic confidence and reducing the risk of recruiting participants who exaggerate or misremember their episodes. However, bed-partner involvement increases recruitment friction — some individuals are unwilling or unable to provide a collateral source, and scheduling joint interviews adds logistical complexity and can slow intake pathway. Over-weighting collateral history may exclude participants with legitimate parasomnias who live alone or whose partners are unavailable.
options: Make bed-partner interview mandatory for all admitted participants; if a participant cannot provide a collateral source, they are eligible only if their pre-admission polysomnography explicitly captured a parasomnia event on record, raising the threshold for solo recruits but ensuring high diagnostic confidence for all admissions.

Conduct bed-partner interviews when available and use the collateral history as a secondary confirmation tool; accept solo recruits based on clinical interview and self-reported frequency alone (≥2 episodes per month), and analyze outcomes stratified by collateral-history availability to assess whether collateral status correlates with event-capture rate or phenotyping robustness.

Develop a brief structured bed-partner questionnaire (10–15 minutes, administered by phone if in-person attendance is infeasible) that captures episode frequency, injury risk, and timing; use questionnaire scores as a pre-screening filter that escalates low-confidence recruits to a senior clinician interview before final admission, balancing accessibility and diagnostic rigor.
levers.review: The core tension is that bed partners provide invaluable collateral history but their involvement is neither uniformly available nor equally reliable — some partners are biased toward exaggeration (liability concerns) or minimization (relationship dynamics), and their absence does not invalidate a genuine parasomnia diagnosis. None of the options explicitly measure the agreement between bed-partner-reported episode frequency and actual captured event rates, risking false assumptions about collateral accuracy.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_classification`: a brief phrase with the category and what the lever decides.
   - Format: "category — what this lever decides".
   - Categories: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - Examples: "governance — who oversees the review process", "methodology — which data collection approach to use", "execution — how to sequence the rollout phases".

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration

Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
